# BitRouter

## Generated File

| File | Description |
|---|---|
| `bitrouter-config.toml` | TOML configuration with per-role agent routing entries |

## How It Works

The `agentroles` command reads your `agentroles.yaml` and produces BitRouter agent-aware gateway config:

```toml

# Generated by agentroles — source of truth: agentroles.yaml

# BitRouter context-aware agentic LLM gateway config

[agents.planner]
name = "planner"
model = "claude-opus-4-8"
provider = "anthropic"
description = """High-stakes reasoning, architecture decisions"""
fallback_models = ["openai/gpt-5.5"]
max_cost = 0.50

[agents.implementer]
name = "implementer"
model = "claude-haiku-4-5"
provider = "anthropic"

[cost_tracking]
enabled = true

[metadata]
tags = ["agentroles"]
tag_calls_with_role = true
```

Each agent entry includes:

- `name` — the role name
- `model` — extracted model ID from primary
- `provider` — extracted provider from primary
- `description` — from `role.notes`
- `fallback_models` — from `role.fallback` (as TOML inline array)
- `max_cost` — from `role.max_cost_per_call_usd`

## Usage

```bash
agentroles build

# Start BitRouter with --config bitrouter-config.toml

```

## Limitations

- The provider/model split assumes `provider/model-id` format — verify against BitRouter's expected provider names
- Fallback models are listed as comma-separated inline TOML array strings, not structured objects
- No auth configuration is generated — BitRouter needs API keys via environment
